title: Other Ways of Seeing the World

required.title: [Read Aloud] Jataka Tales
required.author: retold by Ellen C. Babbitt or similar collection

required.title: [Read Aloud] Prince Siddhartha: The Story of Buddha
required.author: Jonathan Landaw

supplementary.title: [Read Aloud] Stories from the Ramayana and Mahabharata

competence_markers.description: The student can retell the story of the Buddha with specific detail
The student can retell at least two Jataka tales and identify the virtues they teach
The student can explain karma in their own words with a concrete example
The student can describe what Confucius taught about being a good person
The student can explain the Taoist concept of living in harmony with nature
The student is aware that some traditions have many gods, some have no god, and some focus on how to live rather than what to believe
The student can present at least one Eastern tradition's beliefs fairly — the way its followers would present them
The student has tried a simple mindfulness exercise and can describe the experience

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of the Buddha.
narration_prompts.detail: Who was he? What did he discover? How did he teach?
What happened? What virtue was the story teaching?
Explain it in your own words. Give an example from your own life.
What did he teach? How is it different from what the Abrahamic traditions teach?
Why do the Taoists say it cannot be put into words? What does wu wei mean?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick one Eastern tradition and explain what its followers believe.
narration_prompts.detail: How do they answer the big questions? Why do they believe what they believe?

cross_connections.description: The big questions framework introduced in 1.3.1 structures the encounter with Eastern traditions here.
Comparison between Abrahamic and Eastern approaches is a central thread. The student who knows both can see the full range of human answers to the deepest questions.
India and China as cradles of civilization — the historical context for the traditions studied here.
The virtue traditions of Buddhism (compassion, mindfulness), Confucianism (ren, li), and Hinduism (dharma) are studied comparatively in IC1.2.
Buddhist meditation and Taoist practice are contemplative traditions with direct application to emotional regulation.
Yoga and tai chi as embodied practices connecting physical and spiritual traditions.

content.why_this_matters:
You have learned that people all over the world ask the same big questions: Where did we come from? What is right and wrong? What is life for? In 1.3.2, you heard the stories of three great traditions that share the same God — Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.

Now you are going to meet traditions that answer the big questions very differently. These traditions come mostly from Asia — from India, China, and Japan. Some of them have gods — many gods, in fact. Some of them have no god at all. Some of them say the most important thing is not what you believe but how you live. Some say the self you think you are is not real.

These ideas may sound strange. They are not strange to the billions of people who live by them. Hinduism is the world's oldest living religion. Buddhism has shaped the lives of hundreds of millions of people across Asia for over 2,500 years. Confucianism built the civilization of China, Korea, and Japan. These are not small, unusual traditions — they are some of the most influential ways of understanding reality that human beings have ever developed.

You will meet them first through their stories. Every tradition teaches through stories, and Eastern traditions have some of the most beautiful and surprising stories ever told.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Awareness of non-Abrahamic worldviews** — traditions that do not begin with the God of Abraham
2. **The concept of karma** — actions have consequences, even when no one is watching
3. **The idea that the mind can be trained** — that stillness and attention are practices, not just things that happen to you
4. **Respect for nature** as embedded in multiple Eastern traditions
5. **Exposure to Hindu stories** — Krishna, Rama, Ganesha, and the rich narrative world of Hindu tradition
6. **Exposure to Buddhist stories** — the Jataka tales and the life of the Buddha
7. **The idea of wisdom as a way of living** — Confucius and the junzi (the exemplary person)
8. **The concept of harmony** — living in balance with nature, with others, and with yourself
content.core_content:
### Part 1: A World of Many Traditions

When you studied the Abrahamic traditions — Judaism, Christianity, and Islam — you learned about three religions that share the same God, the God of Abraham. They disagree about many things, but they agree about something fundamental: there is one God who created the universe, who cares about human beings, and who has revealed how we should live.

Not all traditions start there. Hinduism has many gods — thousands of them — and also a concept of ultimate reality (Brahman) that is beyond any single god. Buddhism has no creator God at all. Confucianism is not really about gods — it is about how people should live together. Taoism says the deepest truth cannot be put into words.

These traditions are not wrong versions of the Abrahamic traditions. They are different answers to the same deep questions. And for billions of people — more people than the entire population of Europe and the Americas combined — these are the answers that make the most sense.

### Part 2: Hinduism — The World's Oldest Living Religion

Hinduism comes from India. It is at least 4,000 years old — possibly the oldest religion that people still practice today. Unlike Judaism, Christianity, or Islam, Hinduism was not founded by one person. It grew over thousands of years, absorbing many different traditions, stories, and ideas.

**The stories:** Hindu stories are among the richest in the world. You may already know some of them:

*Ganesha* — the elephant-headed god, remover of obstacles, lord of beginnings. Before you start anything important, Hindus pray to Ganesha. Why does he have an elephant head? The story: his father, the great god Shiva, accidentally cut off his head, and to make things right, replaced it with the head of the first creature he found — an elephant. The story is about how even terrible mistakes can lead to something new and wonderful.

*Krishna* — one of the most beloved gods in Hinduism. As a child, Krishna was playful, mischievous, and irresistible. He stole butter from the kitchen and played his flute so beautifully that everyone who heard it stopped what they were doing. As an adult, Krishna became a teacher of profound wisdom. In the great epic the Mahabharata, Krishna counsels the warrior Arjuna on duty, action, and the nature of reality.

*Rama* — the hero of the Ramayana, one of the two great Hindu epics. Rama is the ideal king — brave, just, devoted to dharma (right action). His story — exile, the kidnapping of his wife Sita, the great war to rescue her, his return — has been told and retold for thousands of years across all of South and Southeast Asia.

**The big ideas:** Hinduism teaches that the universe goes through vast cycles — creation, preservation, and destruction, over and over, across billions of years. Everything is connected. Your actions have consequences — not just in this life but across many lives. This is karma. Do good, and good comes to you. Do harm, and harm comes to you. Not as punishment from a judge, but as a natural law, like gravity.

### Part 3: Buddhism — The Path of the Awakened One

About 2,500 years ago, in what is now Nepal, a prince named Siddhartha Gautama grew up in a palace. His father kept him inside the palace walls, surrounded by beauty and comfort, because a wise man had predicted that the prince would either become a great king or a great spiritual teacher.

One day, Siddhartha went outside the palace. He saw four things he had never seen before: an old person, a sick person, a dead person, and a wandering holy man who had given up everything to search for truth. Siddhartha realized that sickness, old age, and death come to everyone — and that all the wealth in the world cannot prevent them.

He left the palace. He spent years searching for the answer to suffering. He tried extreme fasting. He tried intense meditation. He tried following other teachers. Nothing worked.

Finally, sitting under a tree, he resolved not to move until he understood. After a long night of meditation, he broke through. He understood the nature of suffering and the path to its end. From that moment, he was the Buddha — the Awakened One.

**What the Buddha taught:** The Four Noble Truths.

1. **Life involves suffering.** Not that life is only suffering — but that disappointment, loss, sickness, old age, and death are built into the experience of being alive.
2. **Suffering comes from craving.** We suffer because we cling to things that cannot last — pleasure, possessions, even people. Everything changes. Clinging to what changes causes pain.
3. **It is possible to end suffering.** Not by getting everything you want, but by letting go of the craving itself.
4. **There is a path.** The Eightfold Path — right understanding, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.

Notice: the Buddha did not say "believe in me and you will be saved." He said "here is a path. Walk it and see for yourself." Buddhism is unusual among world traditions in that it emphasizes personal practice and direct experience over belief.

### Part 4: The Jataka Tales — Stories of Compassion and Courage

The Jataka tales are stories about the Buddha's previous lives — the lives he lived before the life in which he became the Buddha. In these stories, the future Buddha appears as many different beings — a monkey, a deer, a king, a parrot, a merchant. In each story, he demonstrates a virtue: generosity, patience, courage, compassion, wisdom.

**The Monkey King:** The future Buddha was once a monkey king. When hunters trapped his troop on a cliff, the monkey king stretched his own body across a chasm to form a bridge, letting all the other monkeys cross to safety. He was badly hurt in the process. The human king who witnessed this was so moved that he asked: "Why did you do that?" The monkey king answered: "A leader serves those who follow him."

**The Deer King:** The future Buddha was a golden deer who offered his own life to a king in exchange for the lives of all the other deer in the forest. The king was so impressed by the deer's selflessness that he freed all the deer and declared the forest a sanctuary.

These stories are not about gods or miracles. They are about what it means to be truly good — and they have been told to children across Asia for over two thousand years.

### Part 5: Confucius — The Teacher Who Changed China

About 2,500 years ago, in China, a teacher named Kongzi — known in the West as Confucius — thought deeply about a simple question: how should people live together?

Confucius did not talk about gods or the afterlife. He talked about relationships — between parents and children, between rulers and citizens, between friends. He taught that the good person — the junzi, the exemplary person — cultivates ren (benevolence, kindness) and practices li (proper conduct, respect for others).

**What Confucius taught:**
- **Be kind.** Treat others with genuine care and consideration.
- **Respect your parents and elders.** Not blindly, but because the family is the foundation of all social order.
- **Rulers must be virtuous.** A ruler who is cruel or corrupt has no right to rule.
- **Education matters.** Anyone can become a good person through learning and practice. Goodness is not something you are born with — it is something you cultivate.
- **The Golden Rule (Confucian version):** "Do not impose on others what you do not wish for yourself."

Confucius's teachings shaped China, Korea, Japan, and Vietnam for over 2,500 years. The emphasis on education, respect for elders, and moral self-cultivation that characterizes East Asian cultures has its roots in what this one teacher said in the 6th century BCE.

### Part 6: Taoism — The Way That Cannot Be Named

Around the same time as Confucius, another tradition was developing in China: Taoism (also spelled Daoism). Where Confucius talked about rules, relationships, and social order, the Taoists talked about nature, flow, and letting go.

The central text of Taoism is the Tao Te Ching, attributed to a sage named Laozi. It begins with a paradox: "The Tao that can be spoken is not the true Tao." The deepest truth, Laozi says, cannot be captured in words. It can only be lived.

**Wu wei — non-action:** This does not mean doing nothing. It means acting in harmony with the natural flow of things — like water, which does not force its way but finds the path of least resistance and eventually wears down stone. The Taoist ideal is not effort and control but flexibility and responsiveness.

**Nature as teacher:** Taoism sees the natural world as a source of wisdom. Trees do not try to grow — they just grow. Rivers do not plan their course — they follow the landscape. The Taoist asks: what would it look like to live that naturally? Not fighting against reality but moving with it?

This is a very different approach from the Abrahamic traditions, which often emphasize obedience to divine commands, or from Confucianism, which emphasizes social duty. Taoism says: stop trying so hard. Pay attention. The Way is already there.

### Part 7: Sacred Nature — Shinto and the Living World

In Japan, the oldest tradition is Shinto — a way of seeing the world in which nature is alive with sacred presence. Mountains, rivers, ancient trees, and waterfalls are inhabited by kami — sacred spirits or forces. The kami are not distant gods in heaven. They are here, in this world, in specific places.

Shinto has no founder, no single holy book, and no creed. It is a way of living in respectful relationship with the natural world and with ancestors. At Shinto shrines, people pass through a torii gate — a boundary between ordinary space and sacred space — and make offerings to the kami of that place.

The idea that nature is sacred appears in many traditions — in the Celtic reverence for groves and springs, in the Native American relationship with the land, in the Hindu sense that all of creation is a manifestation of divine reality. Shinto is one of the clearest and most sustained expressions of this idea.

### Part 8: Karma — Actions Have Consequences

One idea appears in several Eastern traditions: karma. The word literally means "action," and the teaching is simple but profound: your actions have consequences. What you do matters — not because someone is watching and keeping score, but because actions create results as naturally as seeds produce plants.

In Hinduism, karma operates across many lifetimes. Good actions lead to a better future life. Harmful actions lead to a more difficult one. This is not punishment from a judge. It is cause and effect.

In Buddhism, karma is about the present as much as the future. Every choice you make shapes the kind of person you are becoming. Choose kindness, and you become a kinder person. Choose anger, and you become an angrier person. You are building yourself with every action.

This idea is not completely different from what other traditions teach — "you reap what you sow" appears in the Bible too. But the Eastern traditions develop it into a central principle: you are responsible for your actions, and your actions shape your reality.

### Part 9: Training the Mind — Meditation and Stillness

Many Eastern traditions teach that the mind can be trained — that you can learn to be calmer, more focused, more aware, and less controlled by your reactions.

**Buddhist meditation:** Sitting quietly, paying attention to your breath, noticing your thoughts without getting carried away by them. The goal is not to stop thinking but to observe thinking — to notice that you are not your thoughts, that thoughts come and go like clouds, and that there is a stillness beneath them.

**Hindu yoga:** Not just the stretching and posing you may have seen — yoga is a system of physical, mental, and spiritual practice aimed at union with the divine. The physical postures are preparation for meditation.

**Taoist practice:** Moving meditation like tai chi — slow, flowing movements that cultivate awareness and harmony between body and mind.

These practices are not magic. They are skills. And modern science has confirmed what Eastern traditions have taught for thousands of years: regular meditation practice changes the brain — it reduces anxiety, improves focus, and increases the capacity for empathy.

### Part 10: Different Glasses, Different World

Remember the invisible glasses from 1.3.1? The Eastern traditions you have learned about in this packet see the world through very different glasses from the Abrahamic traditions.

Where the Abrahamic traditions see one God who created the world and gave humans rules, Hinduism sees a vast divine reality expressing itself through countless forms. Where Christianity sees human nature as fallen and in need of redemption, Buddhism sees human nature as capable of awakening through practice. Where Islam emphasizes submission to God's will, Taoism emphasizes flowing with the natural Way. Where Judaism emphasizes covenant with a personal God, Confucianism emphasizes relationships between people.

None of these traditions is simply right or simply wrong. Each is a serious answer to the deepest questions human beings ask. Each has produced extraordinary civilizations, profound art, and millions of lives lived with purpose and meaning. Understanding them does not threaten your own beliefs — it enriches your understanding of what it means to be human.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Read-Through [Ongoing]
Read the Jataka tales, Hindu stories, and the story of the Buddha aloud. After each, the student narrates back what they heard and discusses: what virtue was this story teaching? What did the characters believe was most important?

### Study 2: The Buddha's Story [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Read the story of Siddhartha Gautama in full. Discuss: Why did he leave the palace? What did he discover? How is his story different from the stories of Abraham or Moses or Muhammad? How is it similar?

### Study 3: Karma Conversations [Mid-Packet]
Discuss karma with the student using concrete examples from their life. If you are kind to someone, what usually happens? If you are mean, what usually happens? Is that karma? How is karma similar to or different from the idea that God rewards good and punishes evil?

### Study 4: Trying Stillness [Mid-Packet]
Practice a simple mindfulness exercise together: sit quietly for two to five minutes, paying attention to breathing. Afterward, discuss: What happened? Was it easy or hard? What did your mind do? The student does not need to become a meditator — but they should have the experience of trying.

### Study 5: Confucius and the Good Person [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Read stories about Confucius and discuss: What does Confucius think makes a good person? How is his answer different from what Judaism or Christianity or Islam says? Is it possible to be a good person without believing in God? This is a real question worth exploring honestly.

### Study 6: Nature as Sacred [Late Packet]
Take the student outside — to a park, a forest, a river, a garden. Discuss: the Taoist idea that nature is a teacher. The Shinto idea that specific places in nature are sacred. The Hindu idea that all of creation is a manifestation of the divine. What do they see when they look at a tree? Can they imagine seeing it the way a Shinto practitioner would — as a place where a kami might dwell?

### Study 7: Comparison Wall [End of Packet]
Add the Eastern traditions to the Big Questions Wall from 1.3.1. For each of the five big questions, add what Hinduism, Buddhism, Confucianism, and Taoism say. The student should now see a wall full of different answers to the same questions — and understand that each answer is held by real people for real reasons.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Story Narration
**Developing well:** The student can retell Eastern stories with specific detail, attributing them to the correct tradition. They remember the Buddha's story, Jataka tales, and stories of Krishna or Rama.
**Developing slowly:** Stories blur together or the student cannot distinguish Eastern from Abrahamic stories.

### 2. Conceptual Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can explain karma, the Tao, and Confucian virtue in their own words, with examples. They understand that these ideas are different from — not inferior to — the ideas in the Abrahamic traditions.
**Developing slowly:** Cannot articulate any Eastern concept in their own words, or consistently frames Eastern ideas as "wrong" versions of Abrahamic ideas.

### 3. Openness Without Confusion
**Developing well:** The student is genuinely interested in how other people see the world. They can hold multiple perspectives without losing their own. "My family believes X, and Buddhists believe Y, and I think that is interesting."
**Developing slowly:** Either distressed by difference ("but which one is true?") or confused into relativism ("I guess everything is true"). Both are normal at this age; neither should be the final state.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you presenting the diversity with calm confidence? The student mirrors your emotional response to difference. If you are comfortable with it, they will become comfortable with it.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### The Exoticism Trap
Eastern traditions are not mysterious or exotic. They are normal to the people who practice them. Do not use words like "strange," "unusual," or "mystical" to describe them. Use the same language you would use for any tradition: "Buddhists believe..." "Hindus practice..." "Confucius taught..."

### Reducing Buddhism to Mindfulness
Modern Western culture has extracted meditation from Buddhism and marketed it as a stress-reduction technique. Buddhism is vastly more than mindfulness. It is a comprehensive worldview with specific claims about the nature of reality, the self, and the path to liberation. The student should encounter Buddhism as a worldview, not as a wellness app.

### Skipping Confucianism Because It Is Not "Religious"
Confucianism does not have gods, miracles, or an afterlife. Some mentors skip it because it does not feel like a religion. Do not skip it. Confucianism shaped East Asian civilization for 2,500 years. It is one of the most influential worldviews in human history. The fact that it achieves this without God or the supernatural is precisely what makes it important in 1.3.

### Treating Hinduism as Polytheism and Stopping There
"Hindus believe in many gods" is like saying "Americans eat food." It is technically true and profoundly inadequate. Hindu theology includes sophisticated philosophical traditions — Advaita Vedanta's claim that all gods are manifestations of one ultimate reality, for example — that rival anything in Western theology for intellectual depth. At Stretching Branches, the student meets the stories and the gods. But do not present Hinduism as a collection of colorful gods with no deeper principle. Even at this age, the student can hear: "Hindus have many gods, and many Hindus believe that all these gods are different faces of one great reality."
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: The Buddha's Story
**Strong:** Retells the story with the key elements — the palace, the four sights, the leaving, the search, the awakening under the tree. Identifies what the Buddha discovered.
**Weak:** "He was a prince who became a monk." No detail, no understanding of what changed.

### Prompt 3: Karma
**Strong:** Explains karma in their own words with a concrete example. May compare it to similar ideas in other traditions.
**Weak:** "It means if you are bad, bad things happen." Oversimplified and punitive — missing the natural-law quality.

### Prompt 6: An Eastern Tradition
**Strong:** Chooses a specific tradition and explains its core ideas in a way the tradition's followers would recognize as fair.
**Weak:** Cannot present any Eastern tradition coherently, or presents a caricature.
